justification: All five pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance identified and solved via Redis Lua compare-and-set script, explicitly rejecting WATCH/MULTI. (2) In-memory Redis state recommended with durability trade-off clearly acknowledged — async projection to PostgreSQL with explicit discussion of the window where Redis has the assignment but PostgreSQL doesn't. (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed with highest-rated courier selection and a Strategy pattern for future flexibility. (4) Hot key risk addressed indirectly — single Redis master handling all bid windows is called out as a rigidity point and risk, with mitigation via Sentinel failover and zone-based partitioning for multi-region. (5) Trade-offs section explicitly covers durability vs. latency: synchronous dual-write rejected in favor of async projection to keep hot-path latency under 50ms.

observations.text: Lua script atomic compare-and-set is the correct primitive — explicitly rejects Redis WATCH/MULTI and PostgreSQL advisory locks with latency reasoning
Thorough durability vs. latency trade-off: async projection to PostgreSQL acknowledged with risk of data loss if both Redis and projection service fail simultaneously
Fallback auto-assignment is well-designed with Strategy pattern, ZSET-based timeout scheduler, and reconciliation job for stuck orders
Clock skew risk addressed by using Redis TIME command as authoritative clock rather than host system clocks
Explicitly warns against database-first approach: 'DO NOT store bid window state in PostgreSQL on the hot path'
Comprehensive risk section covering Redis failover, WebSocket node failure, thundering herd, and projection lag

Hot key problem not called out by that specific name — the single-Redis-master risk is discussed but the scenario of one shard handling all bid windows for a popular zone under Redis Cluster is only implicitly addressed via the decision to use a single master instead of cluster
